questions.text: identify the given clause:Something odd was happening all around the world.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: A subordinate clause cannot stand alone as a complete sentence. It relies on a main clause for its meaning. In this case, 'something odd was happening all around the world' can stand alone, so this option is incorrect.

It is an independent clause because an independent clause is one that can form a complete sentence standing alone, having a subject and a predicate. Here, 'something odd' is the subject and 'was happening all around the world' is the predicate, making complete sense.
A dependent clause cannot stand alone as a complete thought and needs an independent clause to provide context. Since the given clause can stand alone, this option is incorrect.

A relative clause typically begins with a relative pronoun (like 'who', 'that', or 'which') and provides additional information about a noun. Since the clause in question does not fit this definition, this option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: Option B is correct. It is an independent clause because an independent clause can form a complete sentence on its own, containing a subject and a predicate. In the given clause, 'something odd' serves as the subject, while 'was happening all around the world' serves as the predicate, thus making it a complete thought.

questions.text: Identify the following Clause:I do not know what he will do.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: Noun Clause (Correct): A noun clause functions like a noun in a sentence. In this case, "what he will do" acts as the object of the verb "know." It fills the position where a noun would typically go to complete the meaning of the sentence ("I do not know something").
Adjective Clause: An adjective clause modifies a noun or pronoun. There's no noun or pronoun being modified here.

Adverb Clause: An adverb clause modifies a verb, adjective, or another adverb. While "what he will do" describes an action, it doesn't modify the verb "know" in the way an adverb clause typically functions (e.g., "I quickly ran").

Independent Clause: An independent clause expresses a complete thought and can stand alone as a sentence. "What he will do" cannot stand alone as a complete thought because it lacks a subject ("he") and depends on the first part of the sentence for context.

questions.explanation: Noun Clause (Correct): A noun clause functions like a noun in a sentence. In this case, "what he will do" acts as the object of the verb "know." It fills the position where a noun would typically go to complete the meaning of the sentence ("I do not know something").

questions.text: Identify the following Clause:I want to see what had happened.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: Noun Clause (Correct): A noun clause functions like a noun in a sentence. In this case, "what had happened" acts as the direct object of the verb "want to see." It fills the position where a noun would typically go to complete the meaning of the sentence ("I want to see something").
Adjective Clause: An adjective clause modifies a noun or pronoun. There's no noun or pronoun being modified here.

Adverb Clause: An adverb clause modifies a verb, adjective, or another adverb. While "what had happened" describes an event, it doesn't modify the verb "want to see" in the way an adverb clause typically functions (e.g., "I excitedly went to see").

Independent Clause: An independent clause expresses a complete thought and can stand alone as a sentence. "What had happened" cannot stand alone as a complete thought because it lacks a subject and depends on the first part of the sentence for context.

questions.explanation: Noun Clause (Correct): A noun clause functions like a noun in a sentence. In this case, "what had happened" acts as the direct object of the verb "want to see." It fills the position where a noun would typically go to complete the meaning of the sentence ("I want to see something").

questions.text: Identify the following Clause:I expect that I shall get a prize.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: Noun Clause (Correct): A noun clause functions like a noun in a sentence. In this case, "that I shall get a prize" acts as the object of the verb "expect." It fills the position where a noun would typically go to complete the meaning of the sentence ("I expect something").
Adjective Clause: An adjective clause modifies a noun or pronoun. There's no noun or pronoun being modified here.

Adverb Clause: An adverb clause modifies a verb, adjective, or another adverb. While "that I shall get a prize" describes a potential future event, it doesn't modify the verb "expect" in the way an adverb clause typically functions (e.g., "I eagerly awaited the results").

Independent Clause: An independent clause expresses a complete thought and can stand alone as a sentence. "That I shall get a prize" cannot stand alone as a complete thought because it lacks a subject ("I") and depends on the first part of the sentence for context.

questions.explanation: Noun Clause (Correct): A noun clause functions like a noun in a sentence. In this case, "that I shall get a prize" acts as the object of the verb "expect." It fills the position where a noun would typically go to complete the meaning of the sentence ("I expect something").

questions.text: Identify the following Clause:He met a boy whose eyes were blue.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: A noun clause functions like a noun in a sentence. "Whose eyes were blue" doesn't act as a subject, object, predicate nominative, or appositive in this case. It modifies the noun "boy."
An adjective clause modifies a noun or pronoun by providing additional information about it. Here, "whose eyes were blue" describes the boy by specifying his eye color.

An adverb clause modifies a verb, adjective, or another adverb. While the clause describes something ("eyes were blue"), it doesn't modify the verb "met" or any other verb or adverb. It modifies the noun "boy."

An independent clause expresses a complete thought and can stand alone as a sentence. "Whose eyes were blue" cannot stand alone as a complete thought because it lacks a subject and relies on the main clause ("He met a boy") for meaning.

questions.explanation: An adjective clause modifies a noun or pronoun by providing additional information about it. Here, "whose eyes were blue" describes the boy by specifying his eye color.

questions.text: Identify the following Clause:Fatima, whose mom is a nurse, sits behind me in class.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: Noun Clause: A noun clause functions like a noun in a sentence. "Whose mom is a nurse" doesn't function as a subject, object, predicate nominative, or appositive. It modifies the noun "Fatima."

Essential Adjective Clause: An essential adjective clause provides information that is necessary to identify the noun it modifies. In this case, the sentence "Fatima sits behind me in class" still makes sense even if we remove the clause "whose mom is a nurse." Therefore, the clause is not essential for identifying Fatima.

independent Clause: An independent clause expresses a complete thought and can stand alone as a sentence. "Whose mom is a nurse" cannot stand alone as a complete thought because it lacks a subject and relies on the main clause for meaning.

Non-essential Clause (Correct): A non-essential adjective clause provides additional information about a noun or pronoun but is not necessary to identify it. Here, the clause "whose mom is a nurse" gives extra information about Fatima but isn't essential to understand who she is in the context of the sentence.

questions.explanation: Non-essential Clause (Correct): A non-essential adjective clause provides additional information about a noun or pronoun but is not necessary to identify it. Here, the clause "whose mom is a nurse" gives extra information about Fatima but isn't essential to understand who she is in the context of the sentence.

questions.text: Complete the main clause of the following sentence:If I went to Paris, _
questions.subject: English

options.text: I can see the Eiffel Tower
options.explanation: "I can see the Eiffel Tower": "Can" is present tense and does not match the past subjunctive "went." It refers to an ability in the present, not a hypothetical situation.

options.text: I must see the Eiffel Tower
options.explanation: ﻿"I must see the Eiffel Tower": "Must" implies obligation or necessity, which doesn't fit the hypothetical nature of the conditional sentence.

options.text: I should see the Eiffel Tower
options.explanation: "I should see the Eiffel Tower": "Should" implies advice or a recommendation, but it doesn't match well with the past subjunctive form "went."

options.text: I could see the Eiffel Tower
options.explanation: "I could see the Eiffel Tower": While grammatically possible, "could" implies possibility or ability, but "would" fits better for expressing what would actually happen in the hypothetical scenario.

options.text: I would see the Eiffel Tower
options.explanation: "If I went" is a past subjunctive, used to express a hypothetical or unreal condition. The appropriate verb to match this tense is "would", which indicates a possible outcome in that hypothetical situation.

questions.explanation: "If I went" is a past subjunctive, used to express a hypothetical or unreal condition. The appropriate verb to match this tense is "would", which indicates a possible outcome in that hypothetical situation.

questions.text: The underlined part in the sentence adverbial clause of:Although Mehran is hardworking, yet he failed.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: Concession: This is the correct answer. A concessive clause shows a contrast or unexpected result, often introduced by words like "although" or "even though." In this sentence, "Although Mehran is hardworking" shows a contrast to the unexpected outcome, "he failed."

This option is incorrect. A conditional clause presents a situation and its possible outcome, usually introduced by "if" or "unless." For example, "If Mehran is hardworking, he will succeed." This does not match the structure or meaning of the sentence given.

A manner clause describes how something is done, often using words like "as if" or "like." For example, "Mehran worked as if he were tireless." This does not fit because the sentence does not describe how he is hardworking.

A reason clause explains why something happens, often introduced by "because" or "since." For example, "Mehran failed because he lacked support." This does not match the sentence structure, as the sentence does not explain the reason for his failure.

questions.explanation: Concession: This is the correct answer. A concessive clause shows a contrast or unexpected result, often introduced by words like "although" or "even though." In this sentence, "Although Mehran is hardworking" shows a contrast to the unexpected outcome, "he failed."

questions.text: Six o'clock was the time when we were supposed to be there. What type of clause is the underlined part?
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: A noun clause can act as a subject, object, or complement in a sentence. However, in this case, the clause does not serve that function but instead modifies the time.
An adjective clause modifies a noun and provides more information about it. The underlined clause describes 'the time' by indicating when it is, thus functioning as an adjective.

An adverb clause modifies a verb, adjective, or another adverb, explaining when, where, why, or how something happens. The underlined clause does not modify a verb or an adjective; it describes a noun instead.
An independent clause can stand alone as a complete sentence. The underlined clause cannot stand alone; it depends on the main clause for its meaning.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is B: Adjective because the underlined clause 'when we were supposed to be there' modifies the noun 'time' by providing specific information about it. This makes it an adjective clause. Option A is incorrect because, while a noun clause serves as a subject, object, or complement, this clause does not fulfill any of those roles. Option C is incorrect because an adverb clause modifies verbs, adjectives, or other adverbs, and this clause does not perform that function. Lastly, option D is incorrect because an independent clause can stand alone, whereas the underlined clause is dependent on the main clause for its meaning.
